items.title: Rebase locally, merge for landing
items.avoid: Rebasing shared branches; merging your private feature branch onto `main` every few hours.
items.prefer: Rebase your *private* feature branch onto `main` to stay current; use *merge* (or squash-merge) when *landing* the PR.
items.why: Keeps your branch linear and clean while preserving safe semantics for the public history.
items.example:
```bash
# stay current locally
git rebase origin/main
# land via PR's 'Merge' or 'Squash and merge' button
```

items.title: Polish with interactive rebase before the PR
items.avoid: Asking a reviewer to wade through 25 `wip` commits.
items.prefer: `git rebase -i origin/main` and squash / reword to ~3-5 coherent commits.
items.why: Reviewers are scarce — a clean commit series is the single biggest accelerator of PR review time.
items.example:
```bash
git rebase -i origin/main
# squash wip commits; reword unhelpful messages
```

items.title: Use `--force-with-lease`, never `--force`
items.avoid: `git push --force` on any branch other than `feature/MY-OWN`.
items.prefer: `git push --force-with-lease` so the push is rejected if someone else has moved the remote.

items.title: Set `pull.ff = only`
items.avoid: Default `git pull` quietly creating merge commits when local has diverged.
items.prefer: `git config --global pull.ff only` — pull only fast-forwards; you rebase explicitly otherwise.
items.why: Surprise merge commits clutter history and obscure intent.

items.title: Pick a PR merge policy and stick to it
items.avoid: Each engineer picking 'Squash and merge' or 'Rebase and merge' randomly.
items.prefer: Team decides: usually 'Squash and merge' for small features, 'Rebase and merge' for series-of-commits PRs.
items.why: Consistency makes `git log` and `git bisect` predictable.
items.example: Document in `CONTRIBUTING.md`: 'Default to Squash and merge; use Rebase and merge for refactors of >3 logical steps.'
